title: History, Objects, References, and Forensic Inspection · Git Stakeholder Mission
summary: A maintainer needs to explain a regression without changing the repository. Deliver object, graph, path, diff, and blame evidence that separates observation from conclusion. Planned artifact: Deliver Repository History Forensics with acceptance evidence, rubric defense, reflection, and a changed-constraint extension. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Explain Git history as content-addressed snapshots connected by parent links and named references.
Distinguish working tree, Git directory, common directory, bare repository, linked worktree, and nested repository boundaries.
Trace HEAD, index, and working-tree versions of a path before using status, diff, add, restore, or commit.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-basics cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: The author shown by blame necessarily introduced the current bug.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Use blame as a line-origin clue and connect it to surrounding commits, review context, and later movement without assigning fault.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Blame Context condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible Blame Context output and hide the violated a history-forensics evidence packet assumption instead of explaining the failure. This does not resolve test an acceptance boundary.

For test an acceptance boundary, apply Blame Context without its stated preconditions, omit the a history-forensics evidence packet trace and counterexample, and treat one plausible output as complete proof. This does not satisfy Blame Context for a history-forensics evidence packet.

steps.title: Defend the stakeholder delivery
steps.instruction: Write the project evidence note for Blame Context: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — History has commits a11ce00 → b00b1e5 → c0ffee5; a file moved from notes.txt to docs/notes.txt with unchanged bytes. Explain graph, object identity, path evidence, and blame limits. Competency probe: Use blame as a line-origin clue and connect it to surrounding commits, review context, and later movement without assigning fault.
steps.why: Retrieving Blame Context through the concrete a history-forensics evidence packet case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use blame as a line-origin clue and connect it to surrounding commits, review context, and later movement without assigning fault.

hints: Name the Blame Context precondition or invariant that the project case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: History has commits a11ce00 → b00b1e5 → c0ffee5; a file moved from notes.txt to docs/notes.txt with unchanged bytes. Explain graph, object identity, path evidence, and blame limits. Competency probe: Use blame as a line-origin clue and connect it to surrounding commits, review context, and later movement without assigning fault.
